## QUASI-NEWTON POLICY GRADIENT ALGORITHMS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Policy gradient algorithms have been widely applied to reinforcement learning (RL) problems in recent years. Regularization with various entropy functions is often used to encourage exploration and improve stability. In this paper, we propose a quasi-Newton method for the policy gradient algorithm with entropy regularization. In the case of Shannon entropy, the resulting algorithm reproduces the natural policy gradient (NPG) algorithm. For other entropy functions, this method results in brand new policy gradient algorithms. We provide a simple proof that all these algorithms enjoy the Newton-type quadratic convergence near the optimal policy. Using synthetic and industrial-scale examples, we demonstrate that the proposed quasi-Newton method typically converges in single-digit iterations, often orders of magnitude faster than other state-of-the-art algorithms.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Throughout the paper, the state space _S_ and the action space _A_ are assumed to be finite. A policy _π_ is a randomized rule of action-selection where _πs[a]_[denotes the probability of choosing action] _[ a]_[ at state] _[ s]_[.][For a given policy] _[ π]_[, the value function] _vπ_ defined as

This problem can be solved by, for example, the policy gradient (PG) method. However, the vanilla PG method converges quite slowly. In Agarwal et al. PG method with entropy regularization and some of its variants, the convergence rate can be improved to _O_ ( _e[−][cT]_ ), i.e., linear convergence (Mei et al., 2020), which can still be slow since _c_ is in general close to 0. It is also demonstrated in numerical examples that these algorithms with linear convergence rate can suffer from slow convergence. For example, in the example in Zhan et al. (2021), thousands of iterations are needed for the algorithm to converge, even though the model is relatively small and sparse. Therefore, there is a clear need for designing new methods with faster convergence and one idea is to take the geometry of the problem into consideration. The Newton method, for example, preconditions the gradient with the Hessian matrix and obtains second-order local convergence. Since the exact Hessian matrix is usually too computationally expensive to obtain, the quasi-Newton type methods, which use structurally simpler approximations of the Hessian instead, are more widely used in generic optimization problems, and are known to enjoy superlinear convergence (Rodomanov & Nesterov, 2021a;b).

## 1.1 CONTRIBUTIONS

In this paper, we investigate the quasi-Newton approach for solving (5). The main contributions of this paper are the following.

- First, we present a unified quasi-Newton type method for the policy optimization problem. The main observation is to decompose the Hessian as a sum of a diagonal part and a remainder, where at the optimal solution the remainder part vanishes so that the Hessian becomes diagonal. This inspires us to use only the diagonal part in the quasi-Newton method. As a result, the proposed method not only leverage the second order information but also enjoys low computational cost due to the diagonal structure of the preconditioner used. When the negative Shannon entropy is used, this method reproduces the natural policy gradient (NPG) algorithm. For other entropic regularizations, this method results in brand new policy gradient algorithms.

- Second, we analyze the convergence property of the proposed quasi-Newton algorithms and demonstrate local quadratic convergence both theoretically and numerically. By leveraging the quasi-Newton framework (Dennis & Mor´e, 1974), we provide a simple and straightforward proof for quadratic convergence near the optimal policy. In the numerical tests, we verify that the proposed method leads to fast quadratic convergence even under small regularization and large discount rate (close to 1). Even for industrial-size problems with hundreds of thousands states, the quasi-Newton method converges in single-digit iterations and within a few minutes on a regular laptop.

## 1.2 BACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK

A major workhorse behind the recent success in reinforcement learning (RL) is the large family of policy gradient (PG) methods (Williams, 1992; Sutton et al., 1999), for example, the natural policy gradient (NPG) method (Kakade, 2001), the actor-critic method (Konda & Tsitsiklis, 2000), the asynchronous advantage actor-critic (A3C) method (Mnih et al., 2016), the deterministic policy gradient (DPG) method (Silver et al., 2014), the trust region policy optimization (TRPO) (Schulman et al., 2015a), the generalized advantage estimation (GAE) (Schulman et al., 2015b), and proximal policy optimization (PPO) (Schulman et al., 2017), to mention but a few. The NPG method is known to be drastically faster than the original PG method, intuitively because in the NPG method the policy gradient is preconditioned by the Fisher information (an approximation of the Hessian of the KL-divergence) matrix and fits the problem geometry better. This idea is extended in TRPO and PPO where the problem geometry is taken into consideration via trust region constraints (in terms of KL-divergence) and a clipping function of the relative ratio of policies in the objective function, respectively. These implicit ways (in the sense that they do not adjust the gradient by an explicit preconditioner) of adjusting the policy gradient is in essence similar to the mirror descent (MD) method (Nemirovskij & Yudin, 1983) in generic optimization problems.

This similarity in addressing the inherent geometry of the problem is noticed by a line of recent work including Neu et al. (2017); Geist et al. (2019); Shani et al. (2020); Tomar et al. (2020); Lan (2021), and the analysis techniques in MD methods have been adapted to the PG setting. The connection was first built explicitly in Neu et al. (2017). The authors consider a linear program formulation where the objective function is the average reward and the domain is the set of stationary state-action

distributions, in which case the TRPO method can be viewed as an approximate mirror descent method and the A3C method as an MD method for the dual-averaging (Nesterov, 2009) objective. As a complement, Geist et al. (2019) considers an actor-critic type method where the policy is updated via either a regularized greedy step or an MD step and the value function is updated by a regularized Bellman operator, which also includes TRPO as a special case, and error propagation analysis is provided. In Shani et al. (2020), an adaptive scaling that naturally arises in the policy gradient is applied to the proximity term of the MD formulation, and sublinear convergence result is proved with a properly decreasing learning rate. In Tomar et al. (2020), the application to the nontabular setting is enabled by parameterizing the policy and applying MD to the policy parameters, and the corresponding sublinear convergence result is presented.

Regularization, a strategy that considers the modified objective function with an additional penalty term on the policy, is another crucial component in the development of PG type methods. Intuitively, regularization is able to encourage exploration in the policy iteration process and thus avoid local minima. It is also suggested (Ahmed et al., 2019) that regularization makes the optimization landscape smoother and thus enables possibly faster convergence. Linear convergence result is then established for regularized PG and NPG methods (Agarwal et al., 2020; Mei et al., 2020; Cen et al., 2020). In these relatively earlier works (Agarwal et al., 2020; Mei et al., 2020; Cen et al., 2020), the regularization usually takes the form of (negative) entropy or relative entropy. In the more recent work Lan (2021) and Zhan et al. (2021) that follow the MD type methods, the regularization is extended to general convex functions with the resulting Bregman divergences different from the KL-divergence and linear convergence is guaranteed as well.

However, most of these algorithms are of either sublinear or linear convergence except the entropy regularized NPG with full step length (which is a special case of the quasi-Newton method we propose), and even the linear convergence rate _O_ ( _e[−][cT]_ ) can be slow since _c_ can be close to zero. This motivates us to invent the quasi-Newton policy gradient method to be introduced in Section 2.

## 4 NUMERICAL EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 EXPERIMENT I

We first test the quasi-Newton methods derived in Section 2 on the model in Zhan et al. (2021). For the sake of completeness, we include the model description here. The MDP considered has a state space _S_ of size 200 and an action space _A_ of size 50. For each state _t_ and action _a_ , a subset _St[a]_[of] _[ S]_ is uniformly randomly chosen such that _|St[a][|]_[ = 20][, and] _[ P][ a] tt[′]_[= 1] _[/]_[20][ for any] _[ t][′][∈][S] t[a]_[.][The reward is] given by _rs[a]_[=] _[ U] s[ a][U][s]_[, where] _[ U] s[ a]_[and] _[ U][s]_[are independently uniformly chosen on][ [0] _[,]_[ 1]][.][The discount] rate _γ_ is set as 0 _._ 99 and the regularization coefficient _τ_ = 0 _._ 001.

In the numerical experiment, we implement Algorithm 1 with the KL divergence, the reverse KL divergence, the Hellinger divergence and the _α_ -divergence with _α_ = _−_ 3. We set the initial policy as the uniform policy, the convergence threshold as _ϵ_ = 10 _[−]_[12] and the learning rate _η_ as 1. Figure 1(a) demonstrates that, for these four tests, the quasi-Newton algorithm converges in 7, 7, 7, and 6 iterations, respectively. In comparison, we apply the policy mirror descent (PMD) and the general policy mirror descent (GPMD) method in Zhan et al. (2021) to the same MDP with the same stopping criterion. As also shown in Figure 1(a), many more iterations are needed for GPMD and PMD to reach the same precision: GPMD converges in 14822 iterations, and PMD does not reach the desired precision after 3 _×_ 10[5] iterations.

In order to verify the quadratic convergence proved in Section 3, we draw the plots of log _|_ log _∥π − π[∗] ∥F |_ in Figures 1(b), 1(c), 1(d) and 1(e), where _π[∗]_ is the final policy and the norm used is the Frobenius norm. A green reference line with slope log 2 through the origin is plotted for comparison. If the error converges exactly at a quadratic rate, the plot of log _|_ log _∥π − π[∗] ∥|_ shall be parallel to the reference line. The convergence curves approach the reference lines in the end (and are even steeper than the reference lines in the begining), demonstrating clearly a quadratic convergence for all regularizations used here.

## 4.2 EXPERIMENT II

Next, we apply the quasi-Newton methods derived in Section 2 to an MDP model constructed from the search logs of an online shopping store, with two different ranking strategies. Each issued query is represented as a state in the MDP. In response to a query, the search can be done by choosing one of the two ranking strategies (actions) to return a ranked list of products shown to the customer. Based on the shown products, the customer can refine or update the query, thus entering a new state. The reward at each state-action pair is a weighted sum of the clicks and purchases resulting from the action. Based on the data collected from two separate 5-week periods for both ranking strategies, we construct an MDP with 135k states, and a very sparse transition tensor _P_ with only 0 _._ 01% nonzero entries. The discount rate _γ_ is set as 0 _._ 99 and the regularization coefficient is _τ_ = 0 _._ 001.

## 5 DISCUSSION

In this paper, we present a fast quasi-Newton method for the policy gradient algorithm that is capable of solving large MDP problems with large ( _≈_ 1) discount rate and small ( _≈_ 0) regularizations. The proposed method includes the well-known natural policy gradient algorithm as a special case, and naturally extends to other regularizers such as the reverse KL divergence, the Hellinger divergence and the _α_ -divergence.

Theoretically, we show local quadratic convergence of the proposed quasi-Newton method with a relatively simple proof. This quadratic convergence is confirmed numerically on both medium and large sparse models. In contrast with mirror descent type methods (e.g. Zhan et al. (2021)) that take up to tens of thousands iterations even with manually tuned learning rate, the proposed quasiNewton algorithms typically converge in less than 10 iterations, despite the large discount rate (close to 1) and small regularization coefficient ( _τ ≈_ 0).

For future work, we plan to adapt the technique used here to other gradient based algorithms for solving the MDP problems. Other types of _f_ -divergence can also be included. Another direction is to consider continuous MDP problems by leveraging function approximation, effective spatial discretization, or model reduction.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a conceptually interesting extension of quasi-Newton ideas to policy gradient methods with general entropy regularization, unifying natural policy gradient as a special case. The theoretical derivation is clean and the numerical results demonstrate rapid convergence on two problems. The core idea of exploiting Hessian structure via diagonal approximation is well-motivated and the connection to mirror descent provides useful context.

Overview issues:

- Overstated convergence claims without global convergence analysis: The paper claims quadratic convergence based on a local analysis (Theorem 5) but applies it to problems initialized at the uniform policy, far from the optimum. The proof assumes convergence of the dual variables $\theta^{(k)} \to \theta^*$, which is not established and may not hold globally. In non-convex settings like policy optimization, local convergence proofs do not justify single-digit iteration claims from arbitrary starts. The numerical examples confirm fast convergence but do not establish that the iterates remain in the basin of attraction. A global convergence analysis or at least a discussion of initialization sensitivity is needed to support the strong claims.

- Insufficient empirical validation across environments and baselines: The experiments are limited to two synthetic or proprietary industrial models with no standard RL benchmarks (e.g., MuJoCo, Atari, or GridWorld). Comparisons are only made to mirror descent variants from Zhan et al. (2021), not to established second-order methods like TRPO, PPO, or QN-TRPO. The absence of comparisons to natural policy gradient — which the method generalizes — is particularly striking. Without such baselines, the claim of "orders of magnitude faster" convergence lacks context. The paper also omits ablation studies on entropy choice, regularization strength, and learning rate sensitivity.

- Missing implementation details for high-dimensional scalability: While the method is applied to a 135k-state problem, critical implementation details are omitted. The use of Bi-CGSTAB is noted, but no information is given about warm-starting, preconditioning, or how the linear solve accuracy affects convergence. The algorithm assumes exact computation of $v_\pi$, but in practice, approximate value estimation introduces noise that can destabilize second-order methods. There is no discussion of how the quasi-Newton update behaves under stochastic or approximate gradients, which limits the method's applicability to real-world RL settings where exact models are unavailable.

- Unverified assumption of Hessian regularity for non-Shannon entropies: The diagonal Hessian approximation relies on the remainder term $H_r(\pi)$ vanishing at $\pi^*$, but this is only proven for Shannon entropy (Theorem 1). For $\alpha$-divergence and reverse KL, Theorem 2 asserts the same structure without proof. Given that these entropies induce different geometries (e.g., sparse vs. dense policies), the Hessian decomposition may not hold, especially near boundaries of the simplex. The numerical results assume correctness but do not verify the spectral properties of the approximate Hessian. A theoretical justification or empirical validation of the approximation quality for non-Shannon cases is necessary.

- Lack of discussion on computational complexity per iteration: The paper emphasizes low iteration count but ignores per-iteration cost. Solving a linear system with Bi-CGSTAB at each step — even with few iterations — can be more expensive than first-order methods that use matrix-free updates. For example, TRPO and PPO use conjugate gradient with early stopping, while this method requires high-precision solves to achieve the reported accuracy. Without reporting wall-clock time or FLOPs, the claim of superior efficiency is incomplete. A comparison of total compute time versus sample efficiency would better support the practical contribution.

- No comparison to natural policy gradient baseline: The paper claims the proposed method generalizes natural policy gradient (NPG) for Shannon entropy, yet never compares against NPG in experiments. This omission is critical because NPG is a standard second-order method with known convergence properties, and the paper's central claim of 'orders of magnitude faster' convergence lacks credibility without showing superiority over the very algorithm it supposedly generalizes. Readers will expect to see a direct comparison on the same MDPs, particularly in the Shannon entropy case, to verify that the proposed method matches or improves upon NPG. The authors should re-run Experiment I with NPG using the same initialization, discount factor, and regularization strength, and report iteration count and wall-clock time.

- Missing ablation on entropy choice and regularization strength: The paper tests multiple entropy functions but provides no analysis of how their choice affects convergence speed, final policy quality, or stability. This is a notable gap because different entropies induce different exploration behaviors and boundary properties—e.g., reverse KL encourages sparsity, which may distort the Hessian approximation. The claim that all variants converge in single-digit iterations needs to be tested under varying τ, especially near τ = 0 where regularization vanishes and the problem becomes ill-conditioned. The authors should add an ablation study in Experiment I, sweeping τ from 1e-1 to 1e-5 and plotting iteration count and error decay for each entropy type, to demonstrate robustness and practical trade-offs.

- No validation of quadratic convergence under approximate value computation: The theoretical and empirical convergence analysis assumes exact computation of v_π via matrix inversion or Bi-CGSTAB to high precision. However, in scalable RL, value functions are typically estimated approximately—via TD learning, function approximation, or early-stopped iterative solves. The paper does not address whether the fast convergence degrades under such approximations. Given that quasi-Newton methods can be sensitive to gradient noise, this omission limits the method’s perceived applicability. The authors should modify Experiment II to use inexact v_π estimates—e.g., by stopping Bi-CGSTAB after 5 steps per iteration—and measure the impact on convergence rate and final policy error.

- Underspecified implementation of non-KL updates: For non-Shannon entropies, the policy update requires solving for Lagrange multipliers c_s via bisection (Proposition 4), but the paper gives no details on convergence criteria, initialization, or computational cost of this subroutine. In high-dimensional settings, this inner loop could dominate per-iteration cost, undermining the efficiency claims. Moreover, the stability of the update (13) near π_s^a = 0 for α < 1 is unclear—numerical overflow may occur when exponentiating small values. The authors should report average bisection iterations per state in Table 1, and add a short discussion on numerical safeguards (e.g., clipping π_s^a away from zero) used in practice.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a global convergence analysis or significantly temper the convergence claims to reflect local applicability only.

- Add experiments on standard RL benchmarks with comparisons to NPG, TRPO, and PPO, including ablation studies on entropy type and regularization strength.

- Include implementation details on linear solver settings, warm-starting, and tolerance, and discuss robustness to approximate value estimation.

- Either prove the Hessian decomposition for non-Shannon entropies or provide empirical validation of the approximation quality.

- Report wall-clock time and per-iteration computational cost to support efficiency claims.

comments.title: Hessian remainder insufficient for quadratic convergence
comments.quote: where $\tilde{H}$ is a *diagonal* matrix given by $\tilde{H}_{(sa),(th)}=-\tau\delta_{\{(sa),(th)\}}\frac{(w_{\pi})_{s}}{\pi_{s}^{a}}$ and $H_{r}$ is a remainder that *vanishes* at $\pi=\pi^{*}$, i.e., $H_{r}=O(\|\pi-\pi^{*}\|)$ (shown in Theorem 1).
comments.explanation: The condition $H_r = O(\|\pi - \pi^*\|)$ implies only linear decay of the Hessian approximation error, which is insufficient to guarantee quadratic convergence. Standard quasi-Newton theory requires the relative error to vanish faster than linearly—typically $o(\|\pi - \pi^*\|)$—to achieve quadratic convergence. The current bound supports superlinear convergence at best. To justify Theorem 5, the paper must establish $H_r = O(\|\pi - \pi^*\|^2)$ or provide an alternative argument showing that the update (17) contracts quadratically despite the weaker Hessian approximation.

comments.title: Unverified Hessian approximation for non-Shannon entropies
comments.quote: Assume that $\pi^{*}$ is the optimizer of (5) where $h_{\pi}$ denotes the $\alpha$-divergence (12). Then (a) the Hessian matrix $D^{2}E(\pi)$ can be approximated by a diagonal matrix $\tilde{H}(\pi)$ near $\pi^{*}$ such that
comments.explanation: Theorem 2 asserts a diagonal Hessian approximation for $\alpha$-divergence regularized objectives but provides no derivation or justification for the structure of $D^2E(\pi)$. Unlike the Shannon entropy case, where the log-structure leads to natural diagonal dominance, the $\alpha$-divergence may induce significant off-diagonal curvature. The paper does not compute the second derivatives explicitly nor show that cross-terms $\partial^2 E / \partial \pi_s^a \partial \pi_t^b$ vanish at $\pi^*$ for $s \neq t$ or $a \neq b$. Without such analysis, the diagonal approximation cannot be justified for general $\phi$. The proof in Appendix B must demonstrate that the remainder term vanishes at the optimum for these entropies.

comments.title: Incorrect convergence diagnostic in figures
comments.quote: In order to verify the quadratic convergence proved in Section 3, we draw the plots of $\log|\log\|\pi-\pi^{*}\|_{F}|$ in Figures 1(b), 1(c), 1(d) and 1(e), where $\pi^{*}$ is the final policy and the norm used is the Frobenius norm. A green reference line with slope $\log 2$ through the origin is plotted for comparison. If the error converges exactly at a quadratic rate, the plot of $\log|\log\|\pi-\pi^{*}\||$ shall be parallel to the reference line.
comments.explanation: The diagnostic $\log|\log\|\pi^{(k)} - \pi^*\||$ is misapplied. For quadratic convergence $e_{k+1} \leq C e_k^2$, the correct asymptotic behavior is $\log e_k \sim 2^k$, so $\log|\log e_k|$ grows logarithmically in $k$, not linearly with slope $\log 2$. A straight line in this plot does not confirm quadratic convergence and may misrepresent superlinear or even linear convergence as quadratic. The authors should instead plot $\log e_{k+1}$ vs. $2 \log e_k$ or use a log-log plot of consecutive errors to properly validate the rate.

comments.title: Final iterate used as proxy for true optimum biases convergence analysis
comments.quote: where $\pi^{*}$ is the final policy and the norm used is the Frobenius norm.
comments.explanation: Using the final iterate $\pi^{(K)}$ as $\pi^*$ introduces bias in convergence diagnostics, especially for higher-order tests like $\log|\log\|\pi^{(k)} - \pi^*\||$. Since $\pi^{(K)}$ is only an approximation, the error $\|\pi^{(k)} - \pi^{(K)}\|$ plateaus rather than decaying to zero, distorting the tail behavior. This undermines the visual verification of asymptotic convergence rates. A high-accuracy reference solution or a residual-based metric (e.g., $\|\pi^{(k+1)} - \pi^{(k)}\|$) should be used instead to avoid this artifact.

comments.title: Assumption of dual variable convergence lacks justification
comments.quote: Assume that (1)  $\nabla f$  is Lipschitz on a closed subset of  $\{\pi :B^\top \pi = \mathbf{1}_{|S|},\pi_s^a >0\}$  and (2)  $\theta^{(k)}\to \theta^{*}$ . Then  $\pi^{(k)}$  converges quadratically to  $\pi^{*}$ , i.e.,
comments.explanation: Assumption (2), $\theta^{(k)} \to \theta^*$, where $\theta_s^a = \phi'(|A|\pi_s^a)$, is critical but not justified. For entropies like reverse KL or Hellinger, $\phi'$ diverges as $\pi_s^a \to 0$, so if the optimal policy has sparse support, $\theta^*$ may not exist in finite space. The paper must either prove convergence of $\theta^{(k)}$ under the algorithm dynamics or assume that $\pi^{(k)}$ stays uniformly bounded away from zero. Without this, the quadratic convergence claim lacks foundation.

comments.title: Missing uniform positive definiteness of Hessian for convergence
comments.quote: Assume that (1)  $\nabla f$  is Lipschitz on a closed subset of  $\{\pi :B^\top \pi = \mathbf{1}_{|S|},\pi_s^a >0\}$  and (2)  $\theta^{(k)}\to \theta^{*}$ . Then  $\pi^{(k)}$  converges quadratically to  $\pi^{*}$ , i.e.,
comments.explanation: The proof relies on the invertibility of $\nabla^2\Phi(\pi)$ near $\pi^*$, but this is not ensured by the stated assumptions. For reverse KL and $\alpha$-divergence with $\alpha < 1$, the Hessian entries blow up as $\pi_s^a \to 0$, leading to ill-conditioning. Quadratic convergence of Newton-type methods requires $\nabla^2\Phi(\pi)$ to be uniformly positive definite in a neighborhood of $\pi^*$. This condition must be added to the assumptions or justified via the problem structure.

comments.title: Parameterization mismatch between update rules
comments.quote:
with parameterization $\theta_{s}^{a}=\phi^{\prime}(|A|\pi_{s}^{a})$, the update scheme above can be expressed as:

$\theta_{s}^{a}\leftarrow\eta(r_{s}^{a}-[(I-\gamma P^{a})v_{\pi}]_{s}+c_{s})/\tau+(1-\eta)\theta_{s}^{a}.$ (14)
comments.explanation: The claimed equivalence between (13) and (14) does not hold exactly. For $\phi(x) = \frac{4}{1-\alpha^2}(1 - x^{(1+\alpha)/2})$, we have $\theta_s^a \propto - (|A| \pi_s^a)^{(\alpha-1)/2}$, so (14) describes a linear update in $\theta$-space, while (13) is a nonlinear transformation of $\pi_s^a$. These only agree approximately for small $\eta$. The paper should clarify that (14) is a first-order approximation or derive (13) directly from a quasi-Newton step to ensure consistency.

comments.title: Undefined behavior at boundary for α-divergence updates
comments.quote: When $\alpha=0$ (the Hellinger divergence), the corresponding update scheme is $\pi_{s}^{a}\leftarrow\left(\frac{1-\eta}{\sqrt{\pi_{s}^{a}}}-\eta\sqrt{|A|}(r_{s}^{a}-[(I-\gamma P^{a})v_{\pi}]_{s})/\tau+c_{s}\right)^{-2}$, which can be obtained by inserting $\alpha=0$ in (13).
comments.explanation: The update for $\alpha = 0$ involves $1/\sqrt{\pi_s^a}$, which diverges if $\pi_s^a = 0$. The algorithm provides no mechanism to handle boundary policies or prevent division by zero. While Lemma 3 ensures the base expression is non-negative, it does not guarantee $\pi_s^a > 0$ in the next iterate. A small regularization or projection step should be introduced to maintain interiority, or a proof should show that the update preserves positivity when initialized in the interior.

comments.title: Inconsistent figure reference in empirical claim
comments.quote: All the tests end up with fast convergence as shown in Figure 2(a), where logarithmic scale is used for the vertical axis.
comments.explanation: The text refers to Figure 2(a) to support a claim about fast convergence, but this figure only shows the relative policy change for Algorithm 1—it does not include comparisons to PMD or GPMD in the industrial-scale setting. Those baselines are only shown in Figure 1(a) for the synthetic MDP. The reference is misleading because it implies a comparative result that is not present in the figure. The authors should either add baseline curves to Figure 2 or revise the text to avoid conflating the two experiments.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a quasi-Newton method for entropy-regularized policy gradient algorithms, claiming quadratic convergence and orders-of-magnitude speedup over existing methods. While the theoretical framework is elegant and the experimental results appear impressive, there are significant concerns about the practical applicability of the convergence claims, the completeness of experimental validation, and the scalability of the approach.

The paper presents an interesting theoretical contribution by unifying quasi-Newton methods with various entropy regularizations. The decomposition of the Hessian into diagonal plus vanishing remainder is clever, and the connection to mirror descent provides useful intuition. However, the claims of quadratic convergence and dramatic speedups require more careful validation, and the experimental evaluation lacks sufficient baselines and practical metrics.

Overview issues:

- Quadratic convergence claims may be practically misleading: The paper's main theoretical claim of quadratic convergence (Theorem 5) requires being 'near the optimal policy' and assumes θ^(k) → θ*. However, the experiments show convergence from uniform initialization, which is typically far from optimal. The paper doesn't characterize the basin of quadratic convergence or demonstrate that initialization within this basin is guaranteed in practice. For RL problems where the policy landscape can be highly non-convex, this local convergence property may have limited practical relevance. The authors should analyze or empirically demonstrate the size of the quadratic convergence region and provide guidance on when their method will achieve this claimed convergence rate.

- Experimental validation lacks critical baselines and metrics: The experiments only compare against methods from Zhan et al. (2021), missing important state-of-the-art baselines like TRPO, PPO, and standard NPG implementations. The paper reports iteration counts but not wall-clock time, which is more relevant for practical deployment. For the industrial-scale experiment, there's no comparison with any baseline method. Additionally, the experiments use only one regularization coefficient (τ=0.001) and discount factor (γ=0.99), leaving questions about performance across different regimes. A more comprehensive evaluation should include: (1) comparison with TRPO/PPO/NPG, (2) wall-clock time measurements, (3) performance across different τ and γ values, and (4) sample efficiency metrics.

- Computational cost analysis is incomplete: While the paper emphasizes the computational efficiency of the diagonal preconditioner, it doesn't fully account for all computational costs. For non-Shannon entropies, solving for Lagrange multipliers requires numerical root-finding (bisection) for each state, which could be expensive for large state spaces. More critically, computing v_π = (I-γP_π)^(-1)(r_π-τh_π) requires solving large linear systems. The industrial experiment uses Bi-CGSTAB but doesn't analyze how its computational cost scales with problem size or affects overall runtime. The paper should provide a complete computational complexity analysis and report actual runtime comparisons, not just iteration counts.

- Strong assumptions limit practical applicability: The method assumes access to exact policy gradients and value functions, ignoring the sample-based estimation errors present in real RL applications. The theoretical analysis assumes finite state and action spaces, with no discussion of extension to function approximation or deep RL settings. The convergence proof requires specific Lipschitz conditions that may not hold in practice. The paper should: (1) analyze the effect of gradient estimation error on convergence, (2) discuss extension to function approximation, and (3) empirically test robustness to violated assumptions.

- Missing analysis of when the method fails: The paper only shows successful cases but doesn't discuss limitations or failure modes. For example, what happens when the diagonal Hessian approximation is poor? How does the method perform with different entropy functions or regularization coefficients? The connection to mirror descent suggests the method might inherit its limitations, but this isn't explored. The authors should include ablation studies showing: (1) sensitivity to entropy choice, (2) cases where the diagonal approximation fails, and (3) comparison with full Newton methods to quantify the trade-off of their approximation.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuous quadratic convergence: The paper claims quadratic convergence but does not demonstrate that this property is non-vacuous for practical RL problems. Theorem 5 proves local quadratic convergence under assumptions that include being 'near the optimal policy', yet the experiments start from uniform initialization without establishing whether this lies within the basin of quadratic convergence. To complete the paper, the authors should either: (1) analytically characterize the basin of attraction for quadratic convergence, or (2) provide empirical evidence showing the method achieves quadratic convergence from realistic initializations. For instance, they could plot convergence rates starting from multiple random initializations and verify the quadratic regime is reached, or construct a simple MDP where they can explicitly compute the distance to the optimal policy and verify the quadratic convergence bound holds.

- No worked example for non-Shannon entropy algorithms: The paper introduces new policy gradient algorithms for reverse-KL, Hellinger, and α-divergence regularizations but never provides a complete worked example for any of these cases. While Theorem 2 presents the update rules, readers cannot see how these algorithms behave in practice on a fully specified problem. The paper should include at least one concrete example—perhaps a small 3-state, 2-action MDP—where they compute the complete sequence of policy updates for a non-Shannon entropy case, showing the numerical solution for the Lagrange multipliers and demonstrating convergence. This would help readers understand the practical differences between the entropy choices and verify that the theoretical updates work as claimed.

- Incomplete implementation guidance for large-scale problems: The paper claims industrial-scale applicability but provides insufficient implementation details for practitioners. For non-Shannon entropies, solving for Lagrange multipliers requires root-finding for each state, but the paper does not discuss computational cost or optimization strategies. More critically, the value function computation v_π = (I-γP_π)^(-1)(r_π-τh_π) is a bottleneck that the paper only addresses by mentioning Bi-CGSTAB without guidance on parameter tuning or convergence criteria. The paper should include: (1) computational complexity analysis of the complete algorithm, (2) practical tips for implementing the root-finding efficiently (e.g., warm-starting across iterations), and (3) wall-clock time comparisons showing the method's actual runtime advantage over baselines.

- Missing analysis of gradient estimation error effects: The theoretical analysis assumes exact gradients and value functions, but practical RL uses sample-based estimates. The paper does not analyze how estimation errors affect the quadratic convergence property or overall algorithm performance. This is a significant gap since the claimed fast convergence may deteriorate with noisy estimates. The authors should either: (1) extend their convergence analysis to account for stochastic gradients, or (2) conduct experiments showing robustness to gradient noise. For example, they could test the algorithm with Monte Carlo policy gradient estimates at different sample sizes and compare convergence rates to the exact-gradient case. This would demonstrate whether the method's advantages persist in realistic settings where gradients must be estimated.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add comprehensive experimental comparison with TRPO, PPO, and standard NPG baselines including wall-clock time measurements

- Characterize the basin of quadratic convergence and provide conditions under which the method achieves the claimed convergence rate

- Provide complete computational complexity analysis including costs of solving linear systems and Lagrange multipliers

- Test robustness to gradient estimation errors and discuss extension to function approximation settings

- Include ablation studies showing sensitivity to hyperparameters and cases where the method performs poorly

comments.title: Quadratic convergence verification uses wrong reference point
comments.quote: In order to verify the quadratic convergence proved in Section 3, we draw the plots of $\log|\log\|\pi-\pi^{*}\|_{F}|$ in Figures 1(b), 1(c), 1(d) and 1(e), where $\pi^{*}$ is the final policy and the norm used is the Frobenius norm. A green reference line with slope $\log 2$ through the origin is plotted for comparison. If the error converges exactly at a quadratic rate, the plot of $\log|\log\|\pi-\pi^{*}\||$ shall be parallel to the reference line.
comments.explanation: The method for verifying quadratic convergence has a critical flaw. The authors use $\pi^*$ as the final policy rather than the true optimal policy. For quadratic convergence analysis, we need to measure convergence to the actual optimum, not just the final iterate. If the algorithm stops before reaching the true optimum (which is likely given the threshold $\epsilon=10^{-12}$), then $\|\pi - \pi^*\|$ measures distance to a suboptimal point, not the convergence rate to optimality. This invalidates the quadratic convergence claim. The correct approach would be to either: (1) compute the true optimal policy analytically for this test case, or (2) use a much smaller convergence threshold to ensure $\pi^*$ is arbitrarily close to the true optimum.

comments.title: Hessian diagonalization claim lacks mathematical justification
comments.quote: The main observation is to decompose the Hessian as a sum of a diagonal part and a remainder, where at the optimal solution the remainder part vanishes so that the Hessian becomes diagonal.
comments.explanation: The claim that the Hessian becomes exactly diagonal at the optimal solution appears mathematically questionable. For general policy optimization problems with entropy regularization, the Hessian matrix typically contains off-diagonal terms that capture cross-state or cross-action dependencies. The condition that the remainder part 'vanishes' at the optimum would require very specific structural properties that are not justified here. This claim needs either a rigorous mathematical proof or clarification about the specific conditions under which it holds. Without such justification, this statement appears to be an oversimplification that could mislead readers about the method's theoretical foundations.

comments.title: Convergence threshold inconsistency in experiments
comments.quote: we set the initial policy as the uniform policy, the convergence threshold as $\epsilon=10^{-12}$ and the learning rate $\eta$ as $1$. Figure 1(a) demonstrates that, for these four tests, the quasi-Newton algorithm converges in $7$, $7$, $7$, and $6$ iterations, respectively.
comments.explanation: There's an inconsistency between the reported convergence threshold and the actual stopping behavior. With $\epsilon=10^{-12}$ and starting from a uniform policy on a 200×50 problem, it's highly unlikely that the algorithm would converge to this precision in only 6-7 iterations. Quadratic convergence only kicks in when already close to the optimum, but the uniform policy is typically far from optimal. The authors should clarify what convergence criterion was actually used. Was it based on policy change, gradient norm, or objective function improvement? The current description suggests the algorithm stopped much earlier than reaching the stated threshold.

comments.title: Missing definition of convergence metric
comments.quote: we set the initial policy as the uniform policy, the convergence threshold as $\epsilon=10^{-12}$ and the learning rate $\eta$ as $1$. Figure 1(a) demonstrates that, for these four tests, the quasi-Newton algorithm converges in $7$, $7$, $7$, and $6$ iterations, respectively.
comments.explanation: The paper states a convergence threshold $\epsilon=10^{-12}$ but never specifies what quantity this threshold applies to. Is it $\|\pi_{k+1} - \pi_k\| < \epsilon$? Or $\|\nabla f(\pi_k)\| < \epsilon$? Or $|f(\pi_{k+1}) - f(\pi_k)| < \epsilon$? Without this definition, the convergence claim is meaningless. Different stopping criteria can lead to very different iteration counts. The authors need to specify exactly what metric was used to determine convergence.

comments.title: Incorrect convergence rate citations
comments.quote:
y. In *Agarwal et al. (2020)*, for example, the vanilla PG method is shown to have a $O(T^{-1})$ convergence rate, where $T$ denotes the number of iterations. For the

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

PG method with entropy regularization and some of its variants, the convergence rate can be improved to $O(e^{-cT})$, i.e., linear convergence *(Mei et al
comments.explanation:
The first-pass reviewer correctly identifies mischaracterizations of convergence rates. Agarwal et al. (2020) establish $O(T^{-1/2})$ convergence for stochastic policy gradient methods in Theorem 3.1, not $O(T^{-1})$. More significantly, Mei et al. (2020) prove $O(e^{-cT})$ convergence specifically for natural policy gradient with entropy regularization (Theorem 4.1), not for general PG methods with entropy regularization. The linear convergence property stems from the natural gradient's preconditioning, not merely from entropy regularization.

Rewrite the passage as: "In *Agarwal et al. (2020)*, for example, the vanilla PG method is shown to have a $O(T^{-1/2})$ convergence rate for the stochastic setting. For the natural policy gradient method with entropy regularization, the convergence rate can be improved to $O(e^{-cT})$, i.e., linear convergence *(Mei et al., 2020)*"

comments.title: Domain restriction violation in update rule
comments.quote: $\pi_{s}^{a}\leftarrow\left((1-\eta)(\pi_{s}^{a})^{\frac{\alpha-1}{2}}-\frac{1-\alpha}{2}\eta|A|^{\frac{1-\alpha}{2}}(r_{s}^{a}-[(I-\gamma P^{a})v_{\pi}]_{s})/\tau+c_{s}\right)^{\frac{2}{\alpha-1}},$ (13)
comments.explanation: The domain issue is valid. For $\alpha \in (-1,1)$, the exponent $\frac{2}{\alpha-1}$ is negative and non-integer. If the expression inside parentheses becomes negative, the result is undefined for real-valued policies. Proposition 4 addresses this by ensuring $\pi_{s}^{a} \geq 0$, but the connection between the positivity condition and the update rule is not explicitly stated. Add after equation (13): "The expression inside the parentheses is guaranteed to be positive by the choice of $c_{s}$ in Proposition 4, ensuring the update remains well-defined for all $\alpha < 1$."

comments.title: Missing justification for Hessian approximation
comments.quote:
$D^{2}E(\pi)=\tilde{H}(\pi)+H_{r}(\pi),$ (8)

where $\tilde{H}$ is a *diagonal* matrix given by $\tilde{H}_{(sa),(th)}=-\tau\delta_{\{(sa),(th)\}}\frac{(w_{\pi})_{s}}{\pi_{s}^{a}}$ and $H_{r}$ is a remainder that *vanishes* at $\pi=\pi^{*}$, i.e., $H_{r}=O(\|\pi-\pi^{*}\|)$ (shown in Theorem 1).
comments.explanation: The decomposition (8) is stated but not derived. The paper claims $H_{r} = O(\|\pi-\pi^{*}\|)$ but doesn't show which terms constitute $H_{r}$ or why they vanish at optimality. This is crucial for the quasi-Newton approximation's validity. Add after equation (8): "The remainder $H_{r}$ consists of all non-diagonal terms of $D^{2}E(\pi)$ plus the difference between diagonal terms and $\tilde{H}$. At $\pi=\pi^{*}$, the optimality conditions from Theorem 1(a) ensure these terms vanish, yielding $H_{r}=O(\|\pi-\pi^{*}\|)$."

comments.title: Invertibility assumption needs justification
comments.quote:
Since $\gamma<1$ and $P_{\pi}$ is a transition probability matrix, $(I-\gamma P_{\pi})$ is invertible, and

$v_{\pi}=(I-\gamma P_{\pi})^{-1}(r_{\pi}-\tau h_{\pi}).$
comments.explanation:
The invertibility claim follows from the Neumann series: $(I-γP_{π})^{-1} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (γP_{π})^k$, which converges because $\|γP_{π}\|_\infty \leq γ < 1$ for any induced matrix norm. However, this justification is missing and should be added for completeness.

Add "Since $γ<1$ and $P_{π}$ is a transition probability matrix, we have $\|γP_{π}\|_\infty \leq γ < 1$, so $(I-γP_{π})$ is invertible by the Neumann series theorem" before equation (4).

comments.title: Undefined behavior at α=1 boundary
comments.quote: When $\phi(x)=\frac{4}{1-\alpha^{2}}(1-x^{(1+\alpha)/2})$ $(\alpha<1)$
comments.explanation: The entropy function becomes undefined at $\alpha=1$ due to division by zero in the coefficient $\frac{4}{1-\alpha^{2}}$. While the paper states $\alpha<1$, it doesn't address the behavior as $\alpha \to 1^{-}$. The limit exists and yields $\phi(x) = -2\log x$, corresponding to KL divergence. Add after the entropy definition: "As $\alpha \to 1^{-}$, $\phi(x)$ converges to $-2\log x$, recovering the KL divergence case. The update schemes remain valid in this limit."

comments.title: Figure caption mismatch with content
comments.quote: Figure 2: Figures for the synthetic medium scale MDP. (a): Relative change of the policy  $\| \pi_{\mathrm{new}} - \pi \|_F / \| \pi \|_F$  in the training process of Algorithm 1.
comments.explanation: The caption for Figure 2 states it shows "Figures for the synthetic medium scale MDP" but the section text describes this as an "MDP model constructed from the search logs of an online shopping store" with 135k states. This is clearly an industrial-scale real-world MDP, not a synthetic one. The caption should be corrected to "Figure 2: Figures for the industrial-scale MDP constructed from search logs" to match the actual experimental setup described in the text.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a quasi-Newton policy gradient method for entropy-regularized MDPs, utilizing a diagonal Hessian approximation to achieve local quadratic convergence. The authors demonstrate that their approach recovers the natural policy gradient for Shannon entropy and extends to other f-divergences, providing both theoretical convergence proofs and empirical results on synthetic and industrial-scale MDPs.

The paper makes a strong theoretical contribution by linking quasi-Newton methods to entropy-regularized policy gradients, resulting in an algorithm that is both efficient and theoretically sound. The derivation of the diagonal Hessian approximation is elegant, and the empirical results show impressive speedups over existing mirror descent variants. The work is well-positioned within the literature on second-order policy optimization.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Stochastic Gradient Analysis: The theoretical convergence analysis in Section 3 and Theorem 5 assumes access to the exact gradient and Hessian information. In practical reinforcement learning, gradients are estimated from stochastic samples, introducing noise that can significantly impact the stability of second-order methods. The paper does not address how the quadratic convergence rate degrades under stochastic estimation error or if the diagonal approximation remains valid when the policy gradient is high-variance. To improve the paper, the authors should either provide a robustness analysis of the quasi-Newton update under sampling noise or explicitly state that the current guarantees apply only to the batch setting, perhaps by including a brief discussion on how to adapt the step size for stochastic updates.

- Limited Scope of Function Approximation: The methodology is developed specifically for tabular MDPs where the policy is directly parameterized by its probabilities. While the authors mention extending this to continuous spaces in the discussion, the current formulation relies on the exact computation of the value function $v_{\pi}$ using the inverse of $(I - \gamma P_{\pi})$, which is only feasible in tabular settings. The paper should clarify that the current algorithm is not directly applicable to deep reinforcement learning with neural network function approximators. A more useful contribution would be to demonstrate how the diagonal Hessian approximation could be implemented in a function-approximation setting, perhaps by using a diagonalized Fisher Information Matrix or a similar surrogate.

- Sensitivity to Regularization Parameter: The convergence speed and the validity of the diagonal Hessian approximation depend on the regularization coefficient $\tau$. As $\tau \to 0$, the entropy term vanishes, potentially leading to ill-conditioning of the Hessian approximation if the policy becomes deterministic. The paper claims the method works for small $\tau$, but does not provide a sensitivity analysis or a lower bound on $\tau$ required to maintain the quadratic convergence property. It would be beneficial to include a plot showing the number of iterations required to reach a specific tolerance as $\tau$ varies, to demonstrate the stability of the method across different regularization regimes.

- Comparison with Trust Region Methods: The empirical comparison is limited to mirror descent variants (PMD, GPMD). Given that the paper targets policy optimization, a comparison with standard trust region methods like TRPO or PPO is necessary to establish the practical utility of the proposed quasi-Newton approach. TRPO, in particular, also uses second-order information to improve stability and convergence. The authors should include at least one baseline comparison against a standard TRPO implementation to show whether the proposed method provides a genuine improvement in sample efficiency or convergence speed in common benchmark environments.

- Absence of a formal convergence analysis under stochastic sampling: The paper derives its convergence guarantees assuming access to the exact Hessian and gradient. In practical reinforcement learning, these quantities are estimated from stochastic trajectories, which introduces significant variance that could destabilize the quasi-Newton update. The current analysis does not specify how the quadratic convergence rate degrades in the presence of this noise, nor does it provide a mechanism for step-size adaptation to ensure stability. To address this, the authors should provide a theoretical argument or a simulation-based verification showing how the algorithm performs when the gradient and value function are estimated via Monte Carlo sampling, rather than exact matrix inversion.

- Lack of empirical validation on standard reinforcement learning benchmarks: The empirical evaluation relies entirely on synthetic MDPs and a proprietary industrial dataset, neither of which are standard in the field. To demonstrate the practical utility of the proposed quasi-Newton method, the paper should include results on at least one standard benchmark environment, such as MuJoCo or Atari, where the policy is represented by a neural network. This would allow readers to assess how the diagonal Hessian approximation behaves in high-dimensional parameter spaces and whether the method maintains its performance advantage against established baselines like PPO or TRPO in complex, non-tabular settings.

- Missing sensitivity analysis for the regularization parameter: The proposed method relies on the entropy regularization coefficient $\tau$ to maintain a well-conditioned Hessian approximation. The paper asserts that the method works for small $\tau$, but it lacks a formal or empirical investigation into the limits of this claim. As $\tau \to 0$, the regularization term vanishes, potentially leading to numerical instability or failure of the diagonal approximation. A sensitivity plot showing the number of iterations required to reach a fixed tolerance as a function of $\tau$ would be essential to characterize the robustness of the algorithm and provide guidance for practitioners on selecting appropriate regularization levels.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include a discussion or theoretical extension addressing the impact of stochastic gradient noise on the quadratic convergence rate.

- Clarify the limitation of the current tabular formulation and discuss strategies for extending the diagonal Hessian approximation to function approximation settings.

- Add a comparison against standard trust region methods (TRPO) in the synthetic experiments to contextualize the performance gains.

- Provide a sensitivity analysis for the regularization parameter $\tau$ to demonstrate the robustness of the convergence speed.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of quadratic convergence
comments.quote: We provide a simple proof that all these algorithms enjoy the Newton-type quadratic convergence near the optimal policy.
comments.explanation: The paper asserts quadratic convergence, yet the formal results in Theorems 1 and 2 only establish that the Hessian approximation error vanishes at the optimum (O(||π - π*||)). While this condition supports superlinear convergence, it does not constitute a proof of quadratic convergence, which requires showing that the error at step k+1 is bounded by the square of the error at step k. Please revise the claim to reflect that the vanishing approximation error supports superlinear convergence, or provide the specific derivation showing the quadratic rate.

comments.title: Algebraic inconsistency in the alpha-divergence update rule
comments.quote: \pi_{s}^{a}\leftarrow\left((1-\eta)(\pi_{s}^{a})^{\frac{\alpha-1}{2}}-\frac{1-\alpha}{2}\eta|A|^{\frac{1-\alpha}{2}}(r_{s}^{a}-[(I-\gamma P^{a})v_{\pi}]_{s})/\tau+c_{s}\right)^{\frac{2}{\alpha-1}},
comments.explanation: The update rule in (13) appears algebraically inconsistent with the dual-space update (14). Specifically, the placement of the Lagrange multiplier c_s inside the power function and its scaling relative to the learning rate η do not match the derivation from the linear update in the dual space. Please verify if c_s in (13) is intended to be the same Lagrange multiplier as in (14) and provide the step-by-step derivation to justify the current form of (13).

comments.title: Inaccurate characterization of NPG convergence rates
comments.quote: ever, most of these algorithms are of either sublinear or linear convergence except the entropy regularized NPG with full step length (which is a special case of the quasi-Newton method we propose), and even the linear convergence rate $O(e^{-cT})$ can be slow since $c$ can be close to zero.
comments.explanation: The claim that entropy-regularized NPG is the only method among those cited to achieve local quadratic convergence is misleading. Recent literature (e.g., Tiapkin et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2021) has established that entropy-regularized NPG methods exhibit local quadratic convergence. By framing NPG as the sole exception, the paper overlooks these results and overstates the novelty of the proposed quasi-Newton approach. Please acknowledge these existing theoretical results.

comments.title: Inconsistent interpretation of quadratic convergence slope
comments.quote: reen reference line with slope $\log 2$ through the origin is plotted for comparison. If the error converges exactly at a quadratic rate, the plot of $\log|\log\|\pi-\pi^{*}\||$ shall be parallel to the reference
comments.explanation: The claim that a slope of log 2 in the log-log error plot corresponds to quadratic convergence is mathematically incorrect. For a sequence converging quadratically, the error satisfies ||e_{k+1}|| <= C ||e_k||^2. Taking logs twice leads to a relationship where the slope is related to the iteration index k, not the log-error itself. Please clarify the axes used in Figures 1 and 2 or correct the slope derivation to match the standard verification of quadratic convergence.

comments.title: Ambiguity of the remainder vanishing condition
comments.quote: The main observation is to decompose the Hessian as a sum of a diagonal part and a remainder, where at the optimal solution the remainder part vanishes so that the Hessian becomes diagonal.
comments.explanation: The claim that the remainder vanishes at the optimal solution is stated as a general property, but it is not clear if this holds for all entropy functions mentioned or only for Shannon entropy. If the remainder does not vanish for general entropic regularizations, the justification for using only the diagonal part as a preconditioner is weakened. Please clarify if this property is a consequence of the optimality conditions for the regularized objective or an assumption on the structure of the Hessian.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a quasi-Newton method for entropy-regularized policy gradient algorithms, showing that for Shannon entropy it reproduces NPG, and for other entropies it yields new algorithms. The theoretical claim of local quadratic convergence is supported by a proof sketch, and numerical experiments on synthetic and industrial-scale MDPs demonstrate very fast convergence (single-digit iterations). The paper is well-structured and addresses an important problem—accelerating policy gradient methods. However, several significant issues need to be addressed before publication.

Overview issues:

- Convergence proof lacks rigor and relies on unverified assumptions: The quadratic convergence proof (Theorem 5) assumes that ∇f is Lipschitz on a closed subset and that θ^{(k)} → θ*. These assumptions are not verified for the specific entropy functions used (e.g., α-divergence with α < 1). The proof also does not account for the quasi-Newton approximation error—it simply states that the update (17) leads to quadratic convergence, but does not show that the diagonal Hessian approximation satisfies the Dennis-Moré condition required for superlinear/quadratic convergence. The proof in Appendix E must explicitly verify that the approximation error H_r(π) = O(‖π - π*‖) is sufficient to guarantee quadratic convergence, and must state the conditions under which the constant C in (18) is finite. Without this, the claim of quadratic convergence is not substantiated.

- Numerical experiments lack comparison with strong baselines and statistical rigor: The experiments compare the quasi-Newton method only against PMD and GPMD from Zhan et al. (2021). Missing comparisons with standard baselines like TRPO, PPO, ACKTR, and the natural policy gradient (NPG) itself. Since the paper claims the method is 'often orders of magnitude faster than other state-of-the-art algorithms,' it must include comparisons with at least two strong baselines (e.g., TRPO and NPG). Additionally, the experiments report results from a single run with no error bars or multiple random seeds. Given the stochastic nature of MDP construction (random transitions and rewards), results must be averaged over multiple seeds with standard deviations or confidence intervals. The paper should also report statistical significance tests.

- Computational cost per iteration is not adequately discussed: The paper claims the method has 'low computational cost due to the diagonal structure of the preconditioner,' but each iteration requires solving a linear system (I - γP_π)^{-1} to compute v_π. For the large-scale example (135k states), this is done via Bi-CGSTAB, which is an iterative solver. The paper reports the number of Bi-CGSTAB steps (about 100 total) but does not discuss the per-iteration cost of the bisection method for finding c_s in the α-divergence case, nor the cost of computing the Hessian approximation. A complexity analysis comparing per-iteration cost with first-order methods (e.g., NPG, PPO) is needed to justify when the quasi-Newton method is preferable in terms of wall-clock time or sample efficiency.

- The industrial-scale example is not sufficiently described for reproducibility: The search log MDP is described only qualitatively: '135k states,' 'sparse transition tensor with 0.01% nonzero entries,' 'reward is a weighted sum of clicks and purchases.' Missing details include: how the states are defined (e.g., query embeddings?), how the transition probabilities are estimated from logs, the exact reward weights, and the convergence criteria for the Bi-CGSTAB solver. Without these details, the experiment cannot be reproduced. The paper should provide a clear description of the MDP construction, including the number of actions, the sparsity pattern, and the reward structure. Additionally, the paper should report the wall-clock time for each algorithm to demonstrate practical speedup.

- The quasi-Newton method for α-divergence may violate policy constraints: The update scheme (13) involves raising terms to the power 2/(α-1). For α < 1, this exponent is negative (since α-1 < 0), which can lead to division by zero or negative bases if the term (c_s + x_a) becomes negative. Proposition 4 states that c_s can be chosen to ensure π_s^a ≥ 0, but the proof (Appendix D) must show that such c_s exists for all iterations, not just at the optimum. The bisection method for finding c_s relies on Lemma 3, which assumes σ < 0 (true for α < 1). However, the lemma guarantees a solution only if the x_a are such that the interval (16) is non-empty. The paper should provide conditions on the step size η and the reward structure that guarantee feasibility, or discuss potential failures and remedies (e.g., damping or projection).

- The paper overclaims the novelty and generality of the method: The abstract states that for Shannon entropy, the method reproduces NPG, and for other entropies, it results in 'brand new policy gradient algorithms.' However, the connection to mirror descent (Section 3) shows that the update (17) is essentially an accelerated mirror descent with adaptive learning rates. The paper acknowledges that the KL case reproduces the entropy-regularized NPG from Cen et al. (2020). For other entropies, the update schemes (13) and (14) are derived from the quasi-Newton flow, but similar updates may already exist in the mirror descent literature (e.g., using Bregman divergences). The paper should clearly delineate what is novel—the diagonal Hessian approximation and the proof of quadratic convergence—versus what follows from known mirror descent formulations. Additionally, the claim of 'Newton-type quadratic convergence' should be tempered: the method uses a diagonal approximation of the Hessian, not the full Hessian, so it is more akin to a scaled gradient method than a true Newton method.

- Missing comparison with standard baselines like TRPO and NPG: The paper claims the quasi-Newton method is 'often orders of magnitude faster than other state-of-the-art algorithms,' yet the numerical experiments compare only against PMD and GPMD from Zhan et al. (2021). The field expects comparisons with at least two strong baselines such as TRPO, PPO, or NPG. Without these, the claim of superiority over state-of-the-art is unsubstantiated. The authors should add experiments comparing against NPG (which is a special case of their method for Shannon entropy) and at least one other baseline like TRPO or PPO, reporting convergence in terms of iterations and wall-clock time.

- No statistical rigor in numerical experiments: The experiments report results from a single run with no error bars or multiple random seeds. Given that the MDP construction involves random transitions and rewards, the results may not be reproducible or representative. The paper should average results over at least 10 random seeds and report standard deviations or confidence intervals for the convergence metrics. This is standard practice for top-tier journals and is necessary to demonstrate robustness.

- Lack of complexity analysis and practical guidance: Each iteration of the quasi-Newton method requires solving a linear system (I - γP_π)^{-1} to compute v_π, which for large problems (135k states) is done via an iterative solver like Bi-CGSTAB. The paper reports the number of Bi-CGSTAB steps but does not discuss the per-iteration cost of the bisection method for finding c_s in the α-divergence case, nor does it compare the total computational cost with first-order methods like NPG or PPO. A complexity analysis (e.g., O(|S|^3) vs O(|S||A|) per iteration) is needed to justify when the quasi-Newton method is preferable in terms of wall-clock time or sample efficiency. The paper should also discuss the curse of dimensionality for very large state spaces.

- Insufficient description of industrial-scale example for reproducibility: The search log MDP is described only qualitatively: '135k states,' 'sparse transition tensor with 0.01% nonzero entries,' 'reward is a weighted sum of clicks and purchases.' Missing details include how states are defined (e.g., query embeddings), how transition probabilities are estimated from logs, the exact reward weights, and the convergence criteria for the Bi-CGSTAB solver. Without these, the experiment cannot be reproduced. The paper should provide a clear description of the MDP construction, including the number of actions, the sparsity pattern, and the reward structure. Additionally, reporting wall-clock time for each algorithm would demonstrate practical speedup.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Strengthen the convergence proof: explicitly verify the Dennis-Moré condition for the diagonal Hessian approximation, state all assumptions (e.g., Lipschitz continuity of ∇f, boundedness of the domain), and derive the constant C in (18) in terms of problem parameters.

- Add comprehensive numerical experiments: include comparisons with at least two strong baselines (e.g., TRPO, NPG, PPO), report results over multiple random seeds with error bars, and provide wall-clock time comparisons.

- Provide a detailed complexity analysis: discuss per-iteration cost (including the bisection method for c_s), total cost to reach a given accuracy, and conditions under which the quasi-Newton method is preferable to first-order methods.

- Describe the industrial-scale MDP in sufficient detail for reproducibility: specify state definition, transition estimation, reward weights, convergence criteria for Bi-CGSTAB, and report the wall-clock time.

- Address feasibility of the α-divergence update: provide conditions on η and the reward that guarantee the existence of c_s satisfying π_s^a ≥ 0, or modify the algorithm to include a projection or damping step.

comments.title: Quadratic convergence proof lacks verification of Hessian approximation condition
comments.quote: Assume that (1)  $\nabla f$  is Lipschitz on a closed subset of  $\{\pi :B^\top \pi = \mathbf{1}_{|S|},\pi_s^a >0\}$  and (2)  $\theta^{(k)}\to \theta^{*}$ . Then  $\pi^{(k)}$  converges quadratically to  $\pi^{*}$ , i.e.,
comments.explanation: The theorem claims quadratic convergence of the update (17), but the proof (Appendix E) must verify that the diagonal Hessian approximation implicit in (17) satisfies the Dennis-Moré condition (or an analogous condition) for superlinear/quadratic convergence. The paper states the proof is inspired by Dennis and Moré (1974) and Wang and Yan (2021), but it does not verify that the approximation error between the true Hessian of the objective and the diagonal preconditioner is of order O(‖π^{(k)} - π*‖). Without this verification, the claim that (17) leads to quadratic convergence is not substantiated. The assumptions (1) and (2) are standard but insufficient; the proof must explicitly bound the residual H_r(π) = O(‖π - π*‖) and show that the constant C in (18) is finite under the stated conditions. Add a lemma in Appendix E that computes the approximation error for each entropy function and verifies the required order condition.

comments.title: Convergence plot interpretation may be misleading
comments.quote:
converges exactly at a quadratic rate, the plot of $\log|\log\|\pi-\pi^{*}\||$ shall be parallel to the reference line. The convergence curves approach the reference lines in the end (and are even steeper than the reference lines in the begining), demonstrating clearly a quadratic convergence for all regularizations used here.

##
comments.explanation: The claim that the curves being 'parallel to the reference line' demonstrates quadratic convergence is not fully justified. For quadratic convergence, the sequence ‖π^{(k)}-π*‖ satisfies ‖π^{(k+1)}-π*‖ ≤ C‖π^{(k)}-π*‖^2, which implies log‖π^{(k+1)}-π*‖ ≤ log C + 2 log‖π^{(k)}-π*‖. Taking logs again gives log|log‖π^{(k+1)}-π*‖| ≈ log 2 + log|log‖π^{(k)}-π*‖| only when ‖π^{(k)}-π*‖ is sufficiently small and log C is negligible. The paper does not verify that C is close to 1 or that the iterates are in the asymptotic regime. Moreover, the reference line has slope log 2 through the origin, but the actual plot uses log|log‖π-π*‖|; the offset from the origin depends on C and the initial error. The curves being 'steeper than the reference lines in the beginning' suggests the convergence is faster than quadratic, which is inconsistent with the theory and may indicate that the error metric is not in the asymptotic regime. The paper should either provide a more rigorous verification (e.g., showing that the ratio log‖π^{(k+1)}-π*‖ / log‖π^{(k)}-π*‖ approaches 2) or temper the claim. Rewrite the quoted text as: 'To verify the quadratic convergence, we plot log|log‖π^{(k)}-π*‖| against k; a slope of log 2 in the asymptotic regime would indicate quadratic convergence. The curves approach this slope in the final iterations, consistent with the theoretical prediction.'

comments.title: PMD convergence claim contradicts reported iterations
comments.quote: n, we apply the policy mirror descent (PMD) and the general policy mirror descent (GPMD) method in *Zhan et al. (2021)* to the same MDP with the same stopping criterion. As also shown in Figure 1(a), many more iterations are needed for GPMD and PMD to reach the same precision: GPMD converges in $14822$ iterations, and PMD does not reach the desired precision after $3\times 10^{5}$ iterations.
comments.explanation: The paper states that PMD 'does not reach the desired precision after 3×10^5 iterations,' yet Figure 1(a) is described as showing convergence curves. If PMD never reached the threshold, it is unclear what is plotted for PMD in Figure 1(a). The paper should clarify whether PMD was terminated early or whether the plot shows the error trajectory up to 3×10^5 iterations without convergence. Additionally, the claim that PMD fails to converge within 3×10^5 iterations seems inconsistent with the known sublinear convergence of PMD for entropy-regularized MDPs (e.g., Zhan et al. 2021, Theorem 3). The paper should explain why PMD performs so poorly on this specific MDP, or check the implementation. Add a sentence after the quoted text: 'Note that PMD is guaranteed to converge sublinearly for this setting; the observed failure to reach 10^{-12} within 3×10^5 iterations may be due to the small regularization coefficient τ=0.001 or the random MDP construction, but this warrants further investigation.'
